fields.claim: Humans only fit into one of two biological categories, male and female.
fields.details: Biological sex is primarily defined by the production of small mobile gametes (sperm) for males and large immobile gametes (ova) for females, but intersex conditions and chromosomal variations such as XXY, XYY (occurring in about 1 in 1,500 to 1 in 2,000 births), along with variations in chromosomes, gonads, hormones, internal reproductive structures, and external genitals, mean humans do not strictly fit into only two categories. Reliable sources like medical organizations and scientific definitions acknowledge sex as a spectrum or multidimensional construct with variations outside the strict male-female binary.

fields.claim: Surgical procedures cannot change the biological reality of human nature.
fields.details: Biological sex is determined by fixed traits including chromosomes, genes, gamete production, gonads, hormones, and reproductive organs, which surgical procedures alter only superficially (e.g., external genitalia) but do not change fundamentally, such as gamete type or chromosomal makeup. Sources distinguish this from gender, noting doctors can alter physical characteristics but not the core biological definition of sex.

fields.claim: Humans only use 10% of their brain
fields.details: Multiple authoritative sources confirm this is a myth. Brain imaging techniques such as fMRI and PET scans show that large areas of the brain—far more than 10 percent—are active during all sorts of tasks, from rest to complex cognitive work. The entire brain is constantly active even during sleep, regulating, monitoring, sensing, interpreting, reasoning, planning, and acting. Additionally, the brain accounts for approximately 2% of body mass but consumes around 20% of the body's energy (up to 60% for infants), making it far too metabolically expensive to have 90% of it unused. From an evolutionary perspective, if most of the brain were unnecessary, humans with smaller, more efficient brains would have had a significant survival advantage.

fields.claim: The 10% brain myth originated from William James
fields.details: Multiple sources indicate that the origin of the 10% myth is unclear, though it is often attributed to William James and his reserve energy theories from the 1890s. However, sources note that the myth has been misattributed to James—he never specified a percentage but rather proposed that most mental potential goes untapped. The myth may also have originated from misunderstandings of early 20th-century neurological research regarding glial cells and local neurons, or from confusion about brain imaging data interpretation.

fields.claim: A large percentage of teachers and the public believe the 10% brain myth
fields.details: Surveys document widespread belief in this myth. Research found that 43-59% of teachers worldwide reported believing this was true. Hughes et al. (2013) identified the 10% myth as one of the most prevalent misconceptions among the public, and Dekker et al. (2012) found that about 50% of primary and secondary teachers across different cultures endorse the myth. In another survey, 65% of respondents agreed with the statement that people only use 10% of their brain daily.

fields.claim: Goldfish only have a 3-second memory.
fields.details: This is a complete myth with no scientific backing. Goldfish can actually remember for weeks, months, and even years. Research dating back to the 1950s and 1960s has consistently shown goldfish possess much longer memories than 3 seconds. Studies demonstrate that goldfish can learn to associate sounds with feeding, recognize and push colored paddles for food rewards, navigate mazes, and remember these tasks weeks or months later. Dr. Culum Brown, a behavioral ecologist at Macquarie University, explicitly stated: "The three-second memory thing is a complete myth." The misconception may have arisen from the fact that goldfish kept in small circular tanks exhibit repetitive behavior, which was misinterpreted as forgetfulness rather than a response to environmental limitations.

fields.claim: Goldfish only have a 3-second memory.
fields.details: This is a widely debunked myth. Multiple authoritative sources confirm that goldfish have memories lasting far longer than 3 seconds. Research dating back to the 1950s-1960s has consistently demonstrated that goldfish can retain information for weeks, months, and even years. Dr. Culum Brown, a behavioral ecologist at Macquarie University, stated in a 2014 interview that "the three-second memory thing is a complete myth." Studies have shown goldfish can remember feeding locations, learn color preferences, navigate mazes, and recall these tasks weeks or months later. The misconception likely arose from the false assumption that because goldfish lack a hippocampus (the brain structure mammals use for explicit memory), they cannot form memories at all—but research proves this assumption is incorrect.

fields.claim: The current year marks the 200th anniversary of the invention of the camera.

According to authoritative sources, Nicéphore Niépce made the first permanent photograph using a camera in 1826 or 1827, which would place a 200th anniversary around 2026-2027. However, sources also indicate that Niépce began experimenting with fixing camera obscura images in 1816, made a partially successful photograph around 1816, and that 1839 is considered the 'public birthday of photography' when daguerreotype technology was announced. Different reference points (1816, 1826-1827, or 1839) would yield different anniversary years. To verify whether 2026 specifically marks a recognized 200th anniversary, search for official commemorations or anniversaries announced by major photography organizations or institutions.

fields.claim: The device shown in the image is the world's first camera.

However, Niépce had been experimenting with camera obscura devices since 1816. Early cameras were simply smaller versions of the camera obscura. fields.claim: Goldfish have a three-second memory.
fields.details: This claim is false. Extensive scientific research conducted since the 1950s and 1960s has consistently shown that goldfish have much longer memory spans than three seconds. Studies demonstrate that goldfish can retain information for weeks, months, and even years depending on the type of memory and species. For example, goldfish trained to associate sounds with feeding times remembered the connection weeks later. Dr. Culum Brown, a behavioral ecologist at Macquarie University, explicitly stated in a 2014 interview that 'The three-second memory thing is a complete myth.' Goldfish have been successfully trained on various tasks including shock-avoidance exercises, maze navigation, color discrimination, and object recognition, with demonstrated recall of these learned behaviors over extended periods. The myth likely arose from misunderstandings about goldfish brain structure—specifically the absence of a hippocampus—but goldfish use other brain regions such as the pallium for memory formation and spatial cognition.

fields.claim: Forgetting what to say or having slow thinking are symptoms of brain fog.
fields.details: Brain fog is characterized by confusion, forgetfulness, and lack of focus and mental clarity. These symptoms are consistent with experiencing difficulty retrieving words or processing thoughts at normal speed.

fields.claim: Poor sleep quality can cause brain fog even if the duration of sleep is long.
fields.details: Multiple sources confirm that sleep deprivation and lack of sleep cause brain fog, and that adequate sleep of 7-9 hours supports brain function. However, no source explicitly addresses whether poor sleep quality alone, independent of duration, can cause brain fog. The sources discuss sleep duration and sleep patterns but do not directly evaluate the impact of low-quality sleep with sufficient hours. To verify this claim, search for studies on sleep quality versus sleep duration and their effects on cognitive function or brain fog symptoms.

fields.claim: Davao was the safest city in the Philippines and the entire world during Rodrigo Duterte's term.

It was called the 'murder capital' in the 1980s, with death squad killings of at least 1,424 people from 1998-2015. Claims of it being the 9th safest worldwide came from unreliable crowd-sourced site Numbeo, criticized for inaccuracy; police data showed highest murder and second highest rape rates nationally.

fields.claim: No prosecution witnesses have proven that Rodrigo Duterte directly gave orders to kill.
fields.details: Edgar Matobato testified under oath at a 2016 Senate hearing that he was a Davao Death Squad hitman and killed people on Duterte's specific orders, including broadcaster Juan Pala in 2003. Duterte was arrested in 2025 on an ICC warrant for crimes against humanity linked to his drug war, indicating ongoing legal proceedings with witness elements.

fields.claim: The International Criminal Court has no jurisdiction to try former President Rodrigo Duterte for crimes against humanity.
fields.details: The ICC explicitly retains jurisdiction over alleged crimes committed in the Philippines between November 1, 2011, and March 16, 2019, which was the period when the Philippines was a State Party to the Rome Statute. Although the Philippines withdrew from the Rome Statute (effective March 17, 2019), this withdrawal does not eliminate the Court's jurisdiction over crimes committed during the membership period. The ICC Pre-Trial Chamber authorized an investigation on September 15, 2021, and Duterte was surrendered to ICC custody on March 12, 2025. The ICC has directly exercised jurisdiction by accepting Duterte's surrender and finding reasonable grounds to believe he is individually responsible as an indirect co-perpetrator for the crime against humanity of murder. On October 23, 2025, the ICC's Pre-Trial Chamber I dismissed Duterte's challenge to the Court's jurisdiction.

fields.claim: The Philippine legal system has tried and imprisoned thousands of people.

The Philippine drug war context is mentioned, and the country maintains domestic courts with jurisdiction, but the claim about the scale of imprisonments requires verification through criminal justice statistics or official records.

fields.claim: A summer tradition in Southern Italy involves intentionally blocking drains to flood the streets.
fields.details: The sources describe 'A Chiena' as involving the intentional diversion of river water through streets, not the blocking of drains. According to the sources, water from the nearby Tenza River is deliberately rerouted through specially designed canals and a stone dam system (designed approximately 400 years ago) that directs the water through the town center. This is an active redirection of water flow, not a blockage of existing drainage systems.

fields.claim: The festival known as 'A Chiena' takes place in Italy and involves flooding streets for a public water fight.
fields.details: The 'A Chiena' festival is held in the town of Campagna in Salerno, Southern Italy. The festival includes multiple water-related activities, with 'secchiata' being the main afternoon water battle where participants use plastic buckets to splash water on each other in the streets. The event is held annually, and water from the Tenza River is deliberately diverted to flow through the town's streets and main square (Piazza Melchiorre Guerriero). The practice originated in the mid-seventeenth century as a hygienic practice but was revived in 1982-1985 following the 1980 Irpinia earthquake.

fields.claim: The 'A Chiena' festival is an annual event that attracts tourists to Southern Italy.
fields.details: The sources confirm that 'A Chiena' is held every summer on weekends from mid-July to mid-August (with some sources indicating late June to late August). The festival attracts tourists from across the region, particularly from nearby cities like Naples and Salerno, who come to experience this unique local water tradition. The event was formally organized and promoted by the local Pro Loco starting in 1994 after being revived in the early 1980s.

fields.claim: International Criminal Court (ICC) judges walked out during a session involving Filipino lawyer Joel Butuyan.

fields.claim: International Criminal Court (ICC) judges yanked the microphone from Filipino lawyer Joel Butuyan.

fields.claim: Data centers use electricity and water to operate.
fields.details: Data centers use water primarily for cooling their servers and routers, which generate substantial heat during operation. According to a 2024 Lawrence Berkeley National Laboratory report, in 2023 U.S. data centers consumed 17 billion gallons of water directly through cooling systems and an additional 211 billion gallons indirectly through the electricity powering them. A typical 100 megawatt hyperscale data center consumes around 2 million liters of water per day. Data centers are also major electricity consumers, accounting for approximately 4.4% of all U.S. electricity generation in 2023.

fields.claim: The operation of data centers harms communities due to resource consumption.
fields.details: Data center water consumption significantly impacts local communities. A Meta data center in Newton County, Georgia uses 500,000 gallons of water daily, representing 10% of the entire county's water supply. A Google data center in Council Bluffs, Iowa consumed 1 billion gallons of water in 2024, enough to supply all of Iowa's residential water for five days. Bloomberg News reports that two-thirds of new data centers built in the U.S. since 2022 are located in areas already facing water stress. For the proposed Vantage data center in Port Washington, Wisconsin, off-site water use could reach at least 54 million gallons daily—equivalent to the water use of approximately 970,000 Wisconsin residents and more than twice the water used by every home, business, and manufacturer in Green Bay. Beyond water, concerns exist about chemical releases such as PFAS from cooling systems. These consumption levels clearly demonstrate measurable community-level resource impacts.

General context of Sandiganbayan cases exists, but specifics are absent. Suggest searching 'Zaldy Co Sandiganbayan flood control malversation' or 'DPWH Mimaropa officials trial Sandiganbayan'.

fields.claim: The Sandiganbayan initially fined a student P1,000 for a trial disruption and later reduced the fine to P200.
fields.details: Sandiganbayan rules allow fines up to P1,000 for impeding proceedings, but no source confirms an initial P1,000 fine on a student for trial disruption was reduced to P200. The amount matches rules but lacks direct incident report. Suggest searching 'Sandiganbayan student fine reduced P200 phone' or 'Sandiganbayan trial disruption fine P1000 to P200'.

fields.claim: Goldfish do not eat algae.
fields.details: Multiple sources confirm that goldfish do eat algae as part of their omnivorous diet, particularly nibbling on filamentous algae on surfaces, rocks, plants, tank sides, decorations, gravel, and sand. They consume it especially when other food is scarce or as they forage constantly due to lacking stomachs, though they prefer other foods and are not the most effective algae eaters.

fields.claim: Sommarøy is the only place in the world without time.
fields.details: Sommarøy has promoted a symbolic idea of becoming the world’s first "time-free zone," including a 2019 petition by residents to abolish fixed time rules, but this was a publicity concept and is not a legally recognized or literal abolition of time. Multiple sources describe Sommarøy only as a place that "wants to become" or "proposes to be" a time-free zone, not that it already is outside of time. Moreover, many other Arctic and Antarctic locations experience similar daylight patterns, and no authoritative body recognizes Sommarøy as uniquely exempt from timekeeping, so it is not the only place in the world without time.

fields.claim: There is only one sunrise and one sunset a year in Sommarøy.
fields.details: Sommarøy, located north of the Arctic Circle, has a roughly 69–70 day period in summer when the sun does not set (midnight sun), and a winter period when the sun does not rise, but outside these intervals it has normal daily cycles with multiple sunrises and sunsets like other locations. Sources state that the sun stays above the horizon continuously from about 18 May to 22 July, not that there is only one sunrise and one sunset per year. Thus the claim of a single annual sunrise and sunset is incorrect.

fields.claim: The sun never sets for 59 days in Sommarøy between May and July.
fields.details: Authoritative descriptions of Sommarøy’s midnight sun state that the sun remains above the horizon continuously for about 69–70 days, from approximately 18 May to 22 July, not 59 days. One hotel and tourism site for Sommarøy explicitly gives the range 18 May–22 July, which is around 66–70 days depending on how counted, and other descriptions also mention about 69 days. Therefore, while the general phenomenon is correct, the specific figure of 59 days is contradicted by published ranges.

fields.claim: Sommarøy experiences a three-month period of total darkness beginning in November.
fields.details: Sources describe Sommarøy entering the Arctic winter around November, with a period when the sun does not rise, but they do not state a full three-month interval of total darkness beginning in November. Typical polar night durations at Sommarøy’s latitude are on the order of several weeks to roughly two months, and descriptions refer generally to "the long winter" or to the sun not rising during winters, without specifying three full months starting in November. As no authoritative source corroborates a three-month period of total darkness beginning in November and available information suggests a shorter polar night, this specific claim is not supported.

fields.claim: Stores and schools in Sommarøy have no fixed operating hours.
fields.details: Articles discussing Sommarøy’s proposed status as a time-free zone explain that, in theory, life without fixed time would mean stores opening when staff are available and school hours being flexible, and they describe the petitioners’ idea that there would be no clocks and no strict schedules. However, these accounts are about a proposal and a symbolic attitude toward time; they do not document that stores and schools currently have literally no fixed operating hours in practice. Given that Sommarøy is part of Norway’s regular legal and educational systems, it is likely that at least some formal operating hours still exist, but no source explicitly confirms or denies the exact claim. Because there is no direct evidence that all stores and schools truly lack fixed hours, the statement cannot be verified as written. To investigate further, useful search terms would include Sommarøy school operating hours, Sommarøy shop opening times, and Sommarøy time-free zone practical implementation.

fields.claim: Sommarøy, Norway, is the only place in the world without time.
fields.details: Sommarøy follows the standard Norwegian time zone (Europe/Oslo) and uses Central European Time (CET) and Central European Summer Time (CEST), with normal clock changes for daylight saving, so it is not literally a place "without time." Multiple time services list Sommarøy with a defined time zone (CET/UTC+1 in winter, CEST/UTC+2 in summer). The widely reported idea that Sommarøy is a “time-free zone” comes from a tourism and marketing campaign, not from any legal or official abolition of clock time. There is no evidence that Sommarøy is the only place in the world without time in any formal sense; in fact, official records treat it like any other Norwegian locality with a standard time zone.

fields.claim: Sommarøy experiences only one sunrise and one sunset per year.
fields.details: Sommarøy lies above the Arctic Circle and therefore does experience a period of midnight sun in summer and polar night in winter, but it does not have only a single sunrise and a single sunset per year. Astronomical data for Sommarøy show many sunrises and sunsets outside the continuous daylight and continuous darkness periods. During much of the year, the sun rises and sets daily like in other locations, with only certain months having no sunrise or no sunset. No authoritative astronomical source describes Sommarøy as having just one sunrise and one sunset annually, and this would contradict well-understood solar geometry for its latitude.

fields.claim: The sun never sets in Sommarøy for 59 consecutive days between May and July.
fields.details: Sommarøy is just above 69°N, a latitude that does experience a continuous midnight sun period each summer. Nearby Tromsø, at a very similar latitude, has the sun above the horizon without setting for about two months, roughly from mid May to late July. It is therefore plausible that Sommarøy has a continuous no-sunset period of around 60 days spanning parts of May, June, and July. However, the specific figure of exactly 59 consecutive days between May and July is not explicitly confirmed in the available sources for Sommarøy itself. To verify the exact duration and dates, one would need location-specific sunrise and sunset tables for Sommarøy; useful search terms include “Sommarøy midnight sun dates” or “Sommarøy Norway sunrise sunset annual table.”

fields.claim: Sommarøy experiences a three-month long 'Polar Night' starting in November where the sun never rises.
fields.details: At Sommarøy’s Arctic latitude, a polar night period where the sun does not rise above the horizon is expected each winter. Nearby Tromsø (about 69°40′N), which is close in latitude, has a polar night lasting roughly from late November to mid or late January, which is closer to two months than to a full three months. It is plausible that Sommarøy has a similar or slightly different polar night duration, but no precise, location-specific data here state that its sunless period is a full three months starting in November. Without a dedicated sunrise and sunset table specifically for Sommarøy confirming both the start date and the length, the exact claim of a three‑month polar night starting in November cannot be verified. Searching for “Sommarøy polar night length” or “Sommarøy no sunrise dates” in detailed astronomical databases would be needed for confirmation.

fields.claim: Sommarøy has a population of approximately 350 people.
fields.details: One time and location service lists the population of Sommarøy as about 250, not 350, indicating that its population is small but giving a lower approximate figure than the claim. Population on small islands can fluctuate, and different sources may use different boundaries (village, island group, or wider area), so a value around a few hundred is plausible. However, there is no authoritative demographic source here that explicitly confirms a population of approximately 350 for Sommarøy specifically. To verify, one would need recent Norwegian statistical data or municipal records; helpful search terms include “Sommarøy population Statistics Norway” or “Sommarøy i Senja inhabitants.”

fields.claim: Stores and schools in Sommarøy do not have fixed hours.
fields.details: Media coverage has described Sommarøy’s promotional idea of a “time-free zone,” where residents symbolically proposed living without strict clock time. This campaign suggested a relaxed attitude toward schedules, especially during the midnight sun, and some reports mention flexible opening times in a general sense. However, there is no hard evidence that all or most stores and schools in Sommarøy formally operate without fixed hours or timetables year-round. In practice, public services such as schools in Norway normally follow regulated schedules. Existing articles frame the “no time” idea as a tourism or awareness initiative rather than a formally adopted operating model. To confirm any specific businesses or schools without fixed hours, one would need direct documentation or official statements; useful search terms include “Sommarøy school opening hours” or “Sommarøy shops time-free zone practice.”

fields.claim: Purple yam is the best vegetable to fight cancer.
fields.details: Research shows that purple yam (Dioscorea alata) contains polyphenols with anti‑inflammatory, antioxidant, and antitumor effects, and purple yam extracts can reduce colitis-associated colorectal tumor development in animal models. However, authoritative cancer or nutrition guidelines do not identify purple yam as the best vegetable for fighting cancer, and no comparative evidence ranks it above all other vegetables. Therefore, the specific superlative claim that it is the best vegetable to fight cancer is contradicted by the absence of such ranking in the scientific and clinical literature, even though it may have beneficial properties.

fields.claim: Soursop is the best fruit to fight cancer.
fields.details: Laboratory and animal studies have investigated soursop (graviola, Annona muricata) extracts for potential anticancer effects, but major cancer organizations and nutrition guidelines do not recognize soursop as the best fruit to fight cancer, nor is there robust clinical evidence comparing it to all other fruits. The superlative statement is not supported by authoritative sources and is therefore considered false, even though soursop may have bioactive compounds under study. Suggested search terms: "Annona muricata anticancer clinical trials", "graviola cancer evidence".

fields.claim: Nori is the best seaweed to fight cancer.
fields.details: Edible seaweeds, including nori, contain various bioactive compounds and have been studied for potential anticancer and general health benefits, but the literature does not establish nori as the single best seaweed for fighting cancer. No authoritative oncology or nutrition source ranks nori above other seaweeds such as kombu, wakame, or kelp for cancer prevention or treatment. Thus, the specific claim that nori is the best seaweed to fight cancer is not supported by comparative evidence and is considered false. Suggested search terms: "nori seaweed anticancer", "brown vs red seaweed cancer".

fields.claim: Meshima is the best mushroom to fight cancer.
fields.details: Meshima generally refers to Phellinus linteus, a medicinal mushroom studied for potential immunomodulatory and anticancer effects in preclinical research, and some complementary medicine discussions highlight its possible benefits. However, there is no widely accepted, authoritative ranking of mushrooms for cancer prevention or treatment, and strong comparative data versus other medicinal mushrooms such as reishi (Ganoderma lucidum), shiitake (Lentinula edodes), or turkey tail (Trametes versicolor) are lacking. Because direct, authoritative statements that meshima is the best mushroom to fight cancer have not been identified, the claim cannot be verified as written and remains uncertain. Suggested search terms: "Phellinus linteus clinical trial cancer", "medicinal mushrooms comparative anticancer".

fields.claim: Walnut is the best nut to fight cancer.
fields.details: Epidemiologic and experimental studies suggest that walnuts may have chemopreventive properties and could help reduce risk of certain cancers, partly due to their omega‑3 fatty acids and polyphenols. However, major guidelines and scientific reviews describe benefits of nuts in general and do not single out walnuts as definitively the best nut against cancer, nor is there comprehensive comparative evidence proving superiority over almonds, pistachios, hazelnuts, and others. Therefore the specific claim that walnut is the best nut to fight cancer is not supported and is considered false, despite walnuts having potentially beneficial properties. Suggested search terms: "walnut consumption cancer risk", "nuts and cancer prevention".

fields.claim: Quinoa is the best grain to fight cancer.
fields.details: Whole grains, including quinoa, are associated with reduced cancer risk, particularly colorectal cancer, in large observational studies, largely due to fiber and phytochemicals. Nonetheless, no authoritative cancer prevention guideline identifies quinoa as the best grain to fight cancer, and there is no systematic comparative ranking showing quinoa to be superior to other whole grains like oats, barley, or brown rice. The superlative wording is therefore not supported and is considered false. Suggested search terms: "whole grains colorectal cancer risk", "quinoa anticancer properties".

fields.claim: Rosemary is the best herb to fight cancer.
fields.details: Rosemary contains polyphenols such as carnosic acid and rosmarinic acid and has shown antioxidant and antiproliferative effects in cell and animal models of cancer. However, herbal and oncology references do not designate rosemary as the best herb to fight cancer, and robust comparative studies versus other herbs and spices (such as oregano, thyme, or sage) are lacking. Given the absence of authoritative superlative claims or comparative evidence, the statement that rosemary is the best herb to fight cancer is false, despite rosemary having studied bioactive compounds. Suggested search terms: "rosemary extract anticancer", "carnosic acid cancer".

fields.claim: Moringa is the best seed to fight cancer.
fields.details: Moringa oleifera seeds and leaves contain various phytochemicals and have been investigated in vitro and in animals for antioxidant and possible anticancer effects. However, there is no consensus in the scientific or clinical literature that moringa seeds are the best seeds for fighting cancer, and comparative data against other seeds such as flax, chia, or sesame are limited. Because direct authoritative confirmation of the superlative claim is lacking and there is no clear contradictory ranking either, the correct classification under the given rules is uncertain. Suggested search terms: "Moringa oleifera seed anticancer", "moringa clinical trial cancer".

fields.claim: Turmeric is the best root to fight cancer.
fields.details: Turmeric and its principal component curcumin have been extensively studied for anti‑inflammatory and potential anticancer effects, with numerous cell, animal, and early‑phase human studies. Despite this, oncology and nutrition guidelines do not label turmeric as the unequivocal best root to fight cancer, and there is no definitive clinical evidence ranking it above other roots such as ginger or garlic for cancer outcomes. The strong superlative wording is not supported by authoritative comparative data and is therefore considered false, even though turmeric has widely researched bioactivity. Suggested search terms: "curcumin randomized trial cancer", "turmeric chemopreventive evidence".

fields.claim: Butterfly pea flower tea is the best tea to fight cancer.
fields.details: Butterfly pea flower (Clitoria ternatea) contains anthocyanins and other flavonoids, and preliminary laboratory research has explored antioxidant and biological activities, but evidence for direct anticancer effects in humans is sparse. Mainstream cancer resources and nutrition guidelines typically highlight green tea and its catechins for potential cancer‑preventive effects and do not identify butterfly pea flower tea as the best tea for this purpose. Because no authoritative sources could be found explicitly supporting or refuting a superlative ranking for butterfly pea flower tea, and clinical data are very limited, the claim must be rated uncertain. Suggested search terms: "Clitoria ternatea anticancer", "butterfly pea flower tea cancer".

fields.claim: Black seed oil is the best supplement to fight cancer.
fields.details: Black seed oil from Nigella sativa and its compound thymoquinone have demonstrated anticancer and chemopreventive effects in numerous preclinical models, and some early human studies and reviews discuss its potential as an adjunct in cancer care. However, there is no authoritative consensus that black seed oil is the best supplement to fight cancer, and it has not been definitively proven superior to other supplements under investigation, such as vitamin D, omega‑3 fatty acids, or curcumin. Since no clear, authoritative superlative ranking is available but preclinical evidence does show anticancer activity, the correct classification is uncertain rather than strictly false under the provided rules. Suggested search terms: "Nigella sativa thymoquinone cancer", "black seed oil clinical trial oncology".
